Model_Studied: Alongside and freestanding midwifery units vs obstetric units
Key_Finding: No significant difference in adverse perinatal outcomes for low-risk women in midwifery units vs obstetric units. Significantly fewer interventions in midwifery units.
Transfer_Rate_to_Hospital: Nulliparae 36-45%, multiparae 9-13% (alongside). Freestanding higher for nulliparae.
CS_Rate: Lower in MU for equivalent risk
Neonatal_Adverse_Events: No increase in freestanding or alongside unit
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Primary evidence base for freestanding unit safety in UK. Transfer rates for nulliparae are the main planning concern.

Key_Finding: Continuity rates of 40-60% achieved in pilot sites. Staff reported increased job satisfaction. Implementation barriers included roster design and workforce availability.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Implementation lessons relevant to Galway team design.

Model_Studied: Birth centre (inside hospital) vs standard delivery ward
Primary_Outcome: Perinatal mortality, mode of birth, maternal satisfaction
Key_Finding: Perinatal outcomes equivalent. Normal birth rate significantly higher in birth centre group. Maternal satisfaction higher.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Older study but large sample. Co-located model closest to alongside unit, not freestanding.

Primary_Outcome: Maternal and neonatal outcomes, transfer rate
Key_Finding: Low rates of intervention. Intrapartum transfer rate 12.4%. CS rate 6%. No increase in adverse outcomes compared to risk-equivalent hospital births. High maternal satisfaction.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Most directly comparable to the proposed Galway freestanding model. Transfer rate is within international benchmarks.

Primary_Outcome: Transfer rate, mode of birth, neonatal outcomes
Key_Finding: Transfer rate 12.4%, CS rate 5.2%, no increase in adverse neonatal outcomes. Family satisfaction very high. Model financially viable under state Medicaid framework.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Data from author's Boston research exchange cohort. Directly cited in feasibility report.

Model_Studied: Birth centres (alongside and freestanding) vs standard care
Key_Finding: Similar outcomes for low-risk women. Lower rates of epidural, CS, and augmentation in birth centre group.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Australian context, comparable universal healthcare system. Good comparator.

Sample: Subset of Birthplace data plus additional studies
Key_Finding: Transfer rates for nulliparae planned in freestanding MU were higher (36-44%). Transfer rate is a key design variable, not a safety failure.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Transfer rate planning for freestanding units. Key reference for the risk assessment chapter of the feasibility report.

Key_Finding: Named midwife target not met by 2020. Continuity model prioritised but implementation variable across regions. No freestanding birth centres operational outside Dublin by 2025.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Policy context. The gap between strategy and implementation is the central problem this proposal addresses.

Model_Studied: Home birth with midwife vs hospital birth (low-risk)
Primary_Outcome: Perinatal mortality and severe perinatal morbidity
Key_Finding: No significant difference in perinatal mortality or severe morbidity for low-risk women birthing at home with a midwife compared to hospital. Netherlands model is midwife-led community care.
Relevance_to_Galway_Proposal: Home birth evidence. Supports the safety of midwife-attended out-of-hospital birth for low-risk women. Directly relevant to Joan's home birth practice and the transfer protocol design.
